1: Ps 117: The Shortest Chapter in the Bible — Universal Praise, Covenantal Ground. Ps 117 is both the shortest chapter in the Bible (2 verses) and the chapter closest to the Bible's midpoint. Its brevity belies its theological density: in two verses it encompasses universal scope (all nations, all peoples), covenant ground (chesed and emet), and eternal duration ('forever'). Paul cites this psalm in Rom 15:11 as one of four OT texts establishing that Gentiles were always intended to glorify God — the nations praising YHWH is not a NT innovation but a Psalter expectation. 'Praise the LORD, all nations! Extol him, all peoples!' (hallelu et YHWH kol goyim shabechuhu kol haumim) — the imperative extends to 'all nations' (kol goyim) and 'all peoples' (kol haumim). These are universal terms; no nation is excluded from the summons to praise YHWH.
2: 'For great is his steadfast love toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD!' — the grounds for universal praise: YHWH's chesed (steadfast love) 'toward us' (alenu) — the primary recipients are Israel, but the praise is universal. The paradox of Ps 117: the nations are called to praise YHWH because of what he has done for Israel. The particularity of the covenant (YHWH's chesed toward Israel) produces the universality of the praise. 'The faithfulness of the LORD endures forever' (ve-emet YHWH leolam) — the same emet (faithfulness) that appeared in Ps 111-116 closes the Egyptian Hallel's penultimate psalm with the eternal divine attribute. The 'Hallelujah' at the close opens into Ps 118.
